title: Chapter 36: The Aftermath
content: 9:15 PM. The Carrousel du Louvre was pandemonium.

French police had descended in numbers that transformed the sleek underground venue into something between a crime scene and a border crossing. Officers in navy blue moved through the crowd with the practiced calm of people who had seen chaos before but never quite like this — never at Paris Fashion Week, never in a room where the champagne still sat half-finished on lucite trays and the music system was still piping ambient electronica over the screams.

Edward Rochester sat in a folding chair near the northwest service entrance, flanked by two gendarmes. Not arrested. Not yet. "Held for questioning," the officers kept saying, as though the distinction mattered to anyone beyond the legal council already buzzsawing through their phones three meters away. Edward's face was the color of wet cement. He hadn't spoken since the runway. His silence was its own kind of performance — the great designer refusing to acknowledge the stage had been taken from him.

The IPO was dead. BNP Paribas had pulled its underwriting position within the hour. The Euronext listing was suspended pending investigation, which was financial Latin for "we are running in the opposite direction and not looking back." Thornfield's board had emergency-convened a meeting — the kind of meeting where the agenda was a single item and that item was survival.
And the media. God, the media.

The fashion press had been the first wave, but they were now background noise. Financial journalists from the FT and Bloomberg jostled against crime reporters from Le Monde and Le Figaro. International correspondents — CNN, BBC, Al Jazeera, NHK — had set up impromptu camera positions in the Place du Carrousel above, their satellite trucks gridlocking the streets around the Tuileries. A drone circled somewhere overhead, though the airspace was supposed to be restricted. Someone was livestreaming from inside. Everyone was livestreaming from inside.

This wasn't a fashion story anymore. It was a corporate scandal, a human rights case, a feminist reckoning. It was all of them at once, and the weight of that convergence pressed against the walls of the Carrousel like water against a dam.
The green room smelled like lilies and sweat.

It was a small space — a dressing room repurposed for the evening's talent, now repurposed again into something between a sanctuary and a holding cell. The walls were painted an absinthe green that might have been chic in designer lighting but read, under the harsh fluorescents that Jane had insisted on, as the color of illness.

Bertha sat in a folding chair in the center of the room. A paramedic — a young woman with kind hands and a lanyard that read SAMU — was checking her vitals with the careful pacing of someone who understood that the patient was not physically injured but was, in every other sense, in free fall.

Five years without natural light. The paramedic had said it like a diagnosis: photophobia, sensory overload, possible vertigo. Bertha's pupils were struggling to find their calibration. She squinted against even the fluorescent tubes, and when Jane had tried to dim them, Bertha had said no. No more darkness. Not ever again.

The punk jacket was draped over her left arm — not worn, just held, the weight of it pressing into the crook of her elbow like something she was afraid to put down. As if setting it on a chair would mean letting go of the only thing that had felt real in the last forty-seven seconds of her life.

Forty-seven seconds. That's how long she'd spoken on that runway. Jane hadn't timed it — she'd been too busy living inside each word — but the video clips already circulating on social media had a timestamp. Forty-seven seconds from the moment Bertha opened her mouth to the moment the house lights came up and the crowd's roar swallowed everything else.
Five years of silence, ended in forty-seven seconds.

Bertha was shaking. Her whole body trembled in small, continuous waves that didn't look like fear. It looked like a system rebooting — every nerve ending firing at once after years of being chemically muted. The paramedic had offered a mild sedative. Bertha had refused. Jane was glad. A sedative was what Grace Poole would have offered. A sedative was what had kept Bertha in that basement for five years, each dose another layer of silence.
"You're doing fine," the paramedic said, which was a lie, and everyone in the room knew it.
"I'm doing," Bertha said. Which wasn't.

Jane stood near the door, arms crossed, watching. The crimson dress — *her* dress, the one she'd designed for vindication — felt heavier now than it had on the runway. Then it had been armor. Now it was just fabric, and the night wasn't over, and the war she'd started was already becoming something larger than she'd planned.
She could hear the muffled roar of the venue beyond the door. Not applause. Something rawer. The sound of a story breaking apart and reassembling in real time.
Richard arrived at 9:40 PM with two lawyers in tow, and the green room went from sanctuary to command center in the space of a breath.

He looked wrecked — pale, jaw tight, the kind of exhaustion that comes not from fatigue but from the particular nausea of having been right about something terrible. He'd spent the last three hours doing what Richard did best: moving fast, in the right direction, with the right documents.

"I've filed," he said, and the word hung in the room like a gavel strike. "Criminal complaint. False imprisonment, medical abuse, fraud, witness intimidation. Four counts. The French authorities are taking it seriously."
"Because of the video?" Jane asked.

"Because of all of it." Richard pulled a tablet from his bag and set it on the counter. The screen showed a cascade of documents — financial records, medical charts, correspondence. "Video from the show. Financial records showing the payments to Grace Poole, the shell company in Luxembourg that funded the basement renovation, the pharmacy invoices for Bertha's medication — all prescribed by a single physician who, it turns out, hasn't held a valid license in France since 2019. Multiple witnesses — the show attendees, the production crew, Adèle's testimony about what she saw. And the international media coverage means there's no jurisdiction where this can be quietly buried."
Bertha looked up from her chair. "He'll try."
"Of course he'll try." Richard's voice was gentle but unflinching. "But the evidence is irrefutable, Bertha. The world saw it. You can't un-ring that bell."

The paramedic finished her examination and stepped back, giving Bertha a card with follow-up instructions and a look that contained more empathy than any medical professional should have to carry. She left quietly, closing the door behind her.
Bertha's shaking had subsided to a fine tremor in her hands. She still hadn't released the jacket.
"What happens now?" Bertha asked. Not to Richard. To Jane.
Jane opened her mouth, but the door opened again, and this time it was one of the production assistants, breathless, phone clutched like a relay baton.
"You need to see this." She held up the screen. "Blanche Ingram. She's outside. She's talking to the press."
They watched it on Richard's tablet, the livestream stuttering slightly as the cellular network groaned under the weight of Paris trying to watch the same thing at the same time.

Blanche stood on the steps of the Carrousel du Louvre, the glass pyramid glowing behind her like an accusation. Microphones bristled in front of her face — at least eight, maybe ten, representing outlets from five countries. Her hair was still pulled back from the show, severe and elegant, and her eyes were red-rimmed but steady. She looked like a woman who had rehearsed this speech in the mirror for years and was finally, finally delivering it.

"Edward Rochester didn't just abuse one woman," Blanche said, and her voice carried the particular clarity of someone who had been trained to project. "He abused all of us. He reshaped us, rebranded us, and discarded us when we no longer served his narrative."
A pause. Not for effect. Because the next words cost her something.

"I spent three years believing I was his muse." Her jaw tightened. "I was his mannequin. And the woman in the basement? I heard her. I heard her, and I did nothing. I'm here to make sure no one gets to do nothing again."

The live chat on the stream erupted — a waterfall of reactions in six languages. Within minutes, "I heard her and I did nothing" was trending on three platforms. The clip was already being cut, clipped, captioned, and shared with the velocity that only the internet can generate when a moment crystallizes into a movement.

Jane watched Blanche's face on the small screen and felt something complicated move through her — not forgiveness, not yet, but recognition. Blanche had been complicit. Blanche had heard. Blanche had done nothing. And now Blanche was choosing to stand in the light and say so, which was a kind of courage, even if it came too late for the years Bertha had spent in the dark.
"She's brave," Bertha said quietly.
Jane looked at her. "She's late."
"Both can be true."
It was the most Bertha had said since the runway. Jane let it sit.
The phone call came at 10:15 PM.

Richard answered, listened for ninety seconds, and said nothing. His face did something Jane had never seen it do before — it went completely still, the way a chess player's face goes still when the opponent makes a move that wasn't in any opening book.
He hung up. Looked at Jane. Looked at Bertha.
"Edward's lawyers have filed an emergency motion," he said. "They're challenging Bertha's competence."
The room changed temperature.

"They're arguing," Richard continued, his voice careful and controlled, "that a woman who has been declared mentally incompetent — who has a documented diagnosis, who has been on medication for five years, who has medical records from a licensed facility — cannot testify. Cannot bring charges. Cannot stand in a court and be treated as a credible witness."
"That's—" Jane started.

"Legal." Richard cut her off. "It's legal, and it might work. The diagnosis is real — it exists on paper. The medication records exist. Grace Poole will testify that Bertha was unstable. The physician who prescribed the medication — even without a valid license — signed the forms. Edward has 'expert' witnesses lined up. Psychiatrists, neurologists, the whole apparatus. He's been building this defense for years, Jane. He always knew this day might come."
Bertha's hands had stopped trembling. They were perfectly still now, curled around the edges of the punk jacket. Her face was unreadable.
"The French legal system is slow," Richard said. "A competency hearing could take months. During which time, Edward remains free on bail, Thornfield's assets are frozen but not seized, and Bertha—"
"Bertha remains a question mark," Bertha said. Her voice was flat. Not angry. Precise. "Legally speaking. Not a person. A question."

Jane felt something cold settle in her chest. The runway had been so clean — a single, shining moment of irrefutable visibility. But visibility wasn't the same as credibility. The world could see Bertha, but the law could still choose not to believe her. Edward had spent five years constructing the legal architecture to dismiss her, and that architecture didn't crumble just because the lights came up.

She understood, with a clarity that felt like a blade, that the runway had been the easy part. The courtroom was where the real war would be fought. And Edward Rochester — who had spent his entire career navigating contracts and litigation and intellectual property disputes and corporate maneuvering — was more comfortable in a courtroom than he had ever been on a runway.
He hadn't expected the runway. But he'd been preparing for the courtroom since the day he locked the door.
Outside the Carrousel du Louvre, the Paris night was cold.

Jane stood on the edge of the Place du Carrousel, the city spread before her in amber and sodium light. The Tuileries stretched away to the west, their formal gardens ghostly in the darkness, and beyond them the Seine caught the city's glow and threw it back in long, wavering columns. The air smelled of exhaust and rain and the green metallic tang of spring in Paris — something between cut grass and wet stone, impossible to replicate, impossible to forget.

She could still hear the noise from the venue, diminished now to a murmur behind the glass walls. Police radios crackled. A helicopter circled somewhere over the 1st arrondissement. A crowd of onlookers had gathered behind the barricades, phones raised, their screens a constellation of small, hungry lights.

The crimson dress caught the wind and pressed against Jane's legs, the fabric bright as arterial blood against the gray Paris pavement. She had never felt more visible. She had never felt more uncertain if visibility was enough.

Bertha stepped out beside her. The punk tuxedo was too large across the shoulders — it had been designed for a model, not for a woman who had spent five years in a basement and might never have stopped being beautiful, might only have been hidden. The jacket was on now, its weight settling over Bertha's frame like a second skin, and for the first time since the runway, she stood like someone who intended to stay standing.
Two women who had been erased, standing in front of the whole world. Unerased.
Jane's phone buzzed. Then again. Then again. Messages from Richard, arriving in a sequence she didn't need to read to understand:
*He's fighting. His lawyers are good. The competency challenge could take months. We won the PR battle. We haven't won the legal war.*

She held the phone where Bertha could see it. Bertha read the words. A long silence stretched between them — the particular silence that comes after a roar, when the ears are still ringing and the world hasn't yet decided what sound to make next.

"He never expected the runway," Bertha said. Her voice was quiet but steady, the voice of a woman who had spent five years listening and was now choosing when to speak. "He's been preparing for the courtroom for years. That's where he's comfortable."
Jane looked at her. In the low light, Bertha's face was all shadows and angles, but her eyes were sharp. Present. Unmistakably sane.
"Then we change the venue," Jane said. "Again."
Bertha's head tilted. "What do you mean?"

Jane smiled. It was the same smile she'd worn when she said yes to Edward's proposal — that night in the atelier, when the whole world had seemed to narrow to a single point of light and possibility. Real warmth. But now the warmth was weaponized, directed outward like a lamp switched from ambient to searchlight.

"I mean we don't just fight him in court. We fight him in every courtroom, every boardroom, every newsroom, every living room where someone is watching this story and deciding whose side they're on." She paused, and the wind pulled at the crimson fabric, and somewhere behind them a camera flashed. "We've already won the public. Now we make sure the public doesn't forget."

Bertha held her gaze for a long moment. Then, slowly, she pulled the punk jacket tighter around her shoulders and turned to face the lights — the cameras, the city, the witness of the whole watching world.
"Then let's make them remember," she said.

The Paris wind carried the sound away, over the Tuileries, over the Seine, out into the dark where Edward Rochester sat in a folding chair with his silence and his lawyers and the legal architecture he'd spent five years building. A diagnosis on paper. A prescription signed. An expert waiting to testify that the woman in the crimson dress's companion was not a person but a patient — not a witness but a symptom.
The runway was the first battle. The war was just beginning. And Edward Rochester had been fighting legal wars his whole life.
The system he built might protect him still.
